sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: The management of vulvar cancer is complex and requires a multidisciplinary approach tailored to the histologic type (squamous cell carcinoma [SCC], adenocarcinoma, or melanoma), stage, and patient factors. Treatment philosophy emphasizes organ preservation, minimizing morbidity, and optimizing survival outcomes. For SCC and adenocarcinoma, the primary modality is surgery, with radiation therapy (RT) and chemotherapy used in adjuvant, neoadjuvant, or palliative settings. For melanoma, surgical excision is primary, but systemic therapies are increasingly important for advanced disease. All treatment decisions should be made by a multidisciplinary team including gynecologic oncology, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, and radiology [1].

sections.heading: Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) Requirements
sections.content: Multidisciplinary team (MDT) discussion is essential for all patients, especially those with locally advanced or recurrent disease. The MDT should include gynecologic oncology, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, and supportive care specialists. Treatment planning should incorporate patient preferences, performance status, and comorbidities [1, 2].

sections.heading: Performance Status Guide
sections.content: Performance status (PS) influences treatment tolerance. Patients with ECOG PS 0–1 are candidates for aggressive multimodal therapy. For those with PS ≥2, treatment intensity should be modified, focusing on palliation and quality of life. Older adults should be assessed using tools like the Comprehensive Geriatric Assessment (CGA) to guide treatment decisions (see NCCN Guidelines for Older Adult Oncology) [1].

nodes.label: Multidisciplinary Team (MDT) Discussion including gynecologic oncology, radiation oncology, medical oncology, pathology, radiology, supportive care
Surgery with SLNB/Lymphadenectomy ± Adjuvant RT or Chemoradiation (Curative intent)
Systemic therapy, palliative RT, best supportive care (Palliative intent)
Wide excision with SLNB ± Adjuvant systemic therapy (Curative intent)
Systemic therapy, palliative RT, best supportive care (Palliative intent)

edges.label: Localized or locoregional disease (Stages I–IVA) and ECOG PS 0–1
Advanced, recurrent, or metastatic (Stage IVB) or PS ≥2
Unresectable or metastatic disease or PS ≥2

cards.title: Curative
cards.detail: Patient group: Localized or locoregional disease (Stages I–IVA for SCC/adenocarcinoma; Stages I–III for melanoma) Approach: Surgical resection with adequate margins, nodal evaluation via sentinel lymph node biopsy (SLNB) or inguinofemoral lymphadenectomy, and adjuvant RT or chemoradiation based on risk factors. For melanoma, wide excision with SLNB is standard, and adjuvant systemic therapy may be considered for high-risk stages.

cards.title: Palliative
cards.detail: Patient group: Advanced, recurrent, or metastatic disease (Stage IVB for SCC/adenocarcinoma; unresectable or metastatic melanoma) Approach: Systemic therapy, palliative RT for symptom control, and best supportive care. Treatment goals are to prolong survival, relieve symptoms, and maintain quality of life.
